1.synthesis: Paul reminds them of the gospel he preached, which they received, in which they stand, and by which they are being saved—if they hold fast to the word he preached, unless they believed in vain (1 Corinthians 15:1–2). He delivered to them as of first importance what he also received: that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve; then to more than five hundred brothers at one time; then to James, then to all the apostles; last of all to Paul, as to one untimely born (1 Corinthians 15:3–8). By the grace of God he is what he is, and his grace toward Paul was not in vain; he worked harder than any of them—though not he but the grace of God with him. Whether they received it from Paul or from the others, so they believed (1 Corinthians 15:9–11).
1.historical_context: The Christ-event summary of verses 3–5 is one of the earliest written creeds in Christianity, predating all four Gospels. Paul 'received' it (paralambano) and 'delivered' it (paradidomi)—technical terms for the transmission of authoritative tradition in both Jewish and Greco-Roman educational settings. The appearance list (vv.5–8) is Paul's evidence for the resurrection—not a vision but a public appearance to verifiable witnesses, most of whom are still alive ('more than five hundred brothers at one time, most of whom are still alive,' v.6). 'Untimely born' (ektroma, v.8)—possibly a term Paul is quoting from his opponents—refers to a premature or miscarried birth; Paul uses it to describe his violent entry into the apostolate through the Damascus road encounter.
1.literary_structure: The introduction (vv.1–2) establishes the gospel's foundational status; the creed (vv.3–5) provides its content; the appearance list (vv.5–8) provides its historical evidence; the grace-acknowledgment (vv.9–11) situates Paul within the apostolic witness without claiming priority. The purpose of the evidential foundation is to ground the resurrection argument of the chapter's second half: the Corinthians who deny resurrection (v.12) are denying the event that has already been established by multiple witnesses.
1.theological_application: The 'of first importance' language (v.3) establishes a hierarchy within Christian teaching: the death and resurrection of Christ 'in accordance with the Scriptures' is the irreducible core from which everything else derives. The church must constantly return to this center rather than drifting toward secondary matters. The 'if you hold fast' conditional (v.2) is not a threat to assurance but a call to stable confidence in what was received: the gospel does not need to be reinvented in each generation but maintained, tested, and transmitted with fidelity. The witness-list is pastoral as well as apologetic: Paul includes himself last and most humbly, acknowledging that the same gospel he now proclaims was once his target for destruction—evidence that the resurrection's power operates even in the most resistant of hearts, and that grace alone accounts for the transformation.
1.christology: 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures'—the fourfold creed (died / buried / raised / appeared) is the skeleton of all NT christology. The death is atoning ('for our sins'); the burial confirms the reality of the death; the resurrection is third-day and scriptural (echoing Hosea 6:2 and the sign of Jonah); the appearances establish its public, verifiable character. The entire argument of chapter 15 unfolds from this foundation: what the Corinthians have already accepted about Christ's resurrection is the logical basis for their own resurrection, and denial of the latter is implicitly denial of the former. Christ's resurrection is not one datum among others but the event that defines the age, constitutes the church, and grounds every Christian hope.

12.synthesis: If Christ is proclaimed as raised from the dead, how can some of them say there is no resurrection of the dead? If there is no resurrection of the dead, then not even Christ has been raised. And if Christ has not been raised, then Paul's preaching is in vain and their faith is in vain (1 Corinthians 15:12–14). They are then misrepresenting God, because they testified about God that he raised Christ—whom he did not raise if the dead are not raised (1 Corinthians 15:15). If Christ has not been raised, their faith is futile and they are still in their sins. Those who have fallen asleep in Christ have perished (1 Corinthians 15:16–18). If in Christ they only have hope for this life, they are of all people most to be pitied (1 Corinthians 15:19).
12.historical_context: The Corinthian problem was not skepticism about Christ's resurrection but about the resurrection of the dead generally—a Platonic-influenced perspective that valued the soul's immortality and release from the body, not bodily resurrection. Paul's logical reductio demonstrates that the two are inseparable: denying the resurrection of the dead denies Christ's resurrection, which demolishes the entire gospel. The 'still in your sins' (v.17) connects resurrection to atonement: Christ's resurrection is the Father's validation of the cross's effectiveness; without it, the sacrifice has not been accepted and sins remain unforgiven. The 'most to be pitied' (v.19) is the existential consequence: a Christianity that ends at death and offers only this-worldly hope is the cruelest fraud—it demands self-sacrifice for a reward that does not exist.
12.literary_structure: The passage is a sustained hypothetical: if there is no resurrection, then... (vv.13–19). The if-then chain has five consequences: Christ not raised (v.13) → preaching vain (v.14) → faith vain (v.14) → apostles liars about God (v.15) → still in sins (v.17) → dead believers perished (v.18) → all most pitiable (v.19). The accumulating consequences of the denial are designed to show the Corinthians how much they lose if they pursue their partial skepticism. The argument is not primarily apologetic (proving the resurrection to skeptics) but pastoral (showing believers why the resurrection matters).
12.theological_application: The consequences Paul draws from the hypothetical denial of resurrection are the most bracing theological argument in the letter. If the resurrection is false, Christianity is a fraud at its core: not merely mistaken on a secondary point but fraudulent about its central claim, its foundational event, its primary hope. The church must not allow the resurrection to become a metaphor, a spiritual truth symbolized by physical imagery but not requiring actual bodily resurrection. The entire NT is committed to the bodily resurrection of Jesus and the bodily resurrection of those who belong to him, and Paul's argument here makes clear that this commitment is not incidental but load-bearing for everything else. The church's hope does not end at death but triumphs over it—and if it does not, there is no gospel.
12.christology: Christ's resurrection is the hinge of Paul's entire theological system. The creed of verses 3–5 that began the chapter grounds the argument: the Corinthians accepted Christ's resurrection; they must now accept what it entails. Without the resurrection, Christ is still dead and sin still wins—the cross's work is unratified and the promised Spirit undelivered. Paul's logic is inexorable: the God who raised Jesus is the God who will raise those who belong to Jesus, because they are raised 'in Christ' (the phrase that occurs throughout ch.15 as the sphere of resurrection). The resurrection of the dead is not an addendum to Christology but its eschatological completion: what happened to Christ at Easter is what will happen to all who are his when he returns.

20.synthesis: But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep (1 Corinthians 15:20). For as by a man came death, by a man has come also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive (1 Corinthians 15:21–22). But each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ (1 Corinthians 15:23). Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power. For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Corinthians 15:24–26). 'He has put all things under his feet'—but when it says all things are put under him, it is plain that he is excepted who put all things under him. When all things are subjected to him, then the Son himself will also be subjected to him who put all things in subjection under him, that God may be all in all (1 Corinthians 15:27–28).
20.historical_context: The 'firstfruits' (aparche) image is drawn from the Levitical firstfruits offering: the first sheaf of the harvest, consecrated to God and offered as pledge of the full harvest to follow (Leviticus 23:9–14). Christ's resurrection is the first sheaf; the resurrection of believers is the full harvest. The Adam-Christ typology (Romans 5:12–21) is the theological framework: two representative human beings, each initiating a sequence that follows from their act—Adam's sin initiates death for all; Christ's resurrection initiates life for all who belong to him. The Psalm 8:6 / Psalm 110:1 citations (vv.25–27) identify Christ's current reign as the subjugation of all enemies, culminating in death's destruction at the parousia. The trinitarian subordination of v.28 ('the Son himself will be subject to the Father') is an eschatological statement about the consummation, not an ontological statement about eternal hierarchy.
20.literary_structure: The 'but in fact' (nyni de, v.20) reverses the hypothetical argument of vv.12–19 with the actual reality: Christ is risen. The resurrection-sequence (Christ firstfruits → believers at parousia → the end, vv.23–24) provides the eschatological timeline. The enemy-subjugation argument (vv.25–27) identifies the content of Christ's current reign and its ultimate goal. The Son's eschatological self-subjection (v.28) provides the trinitarian goal: God all in all. This is not diminishment but consummation—the final state of a universe fully reconciled to its Creator.
20.theological_application: The Adam-Christ typology establishes that death is not part of the original good creation but an alien intrusion that the resurrection reverses. 'In Adam all die' is the diagnosis; 'in Christ shall all be made alive' is the cure—and it is as universal as the disease. The 'each in his own order' qualification specifies the scope of 'all': Christ first, then those who belong to Christ at his coming. This is resurrection-participation by union with Christ, not universal resurrection regardless of relationship with him. The 'last enemy to be destroyed is death' (v.26) gives the church its ultimate horizon: every present struggle is sub-eschatological; death is the final challenge and the resurrection is its answer. The church that lives within this eschatological framework takes both present suffering seriously (it is real) and treats it with confident hope (death does not have the final word).
20.christology: Christ's current reign ('he must reign until he has put all enemies under his feet,' v.25) is the reality of the present age: the risen Lord is not passive but actively governing the universe toward the goal of death's destruction and the creation's full submission to the Creator. The 'firstfruits' christology positions the resurrection not as an isolated miracle but as the eschatological beginning of a sequence that encompasses all creation: the resurrection of Jesus is not merely personal vindication but the turn of the ages, the beginning of the new creation. The eschatological self-subjection of v.28—'that God may be all in all'—is the goal of the entire christological mission: the Son's work is complete when the creation is fully submitted to the Father through whom it came and for whom it exists. This is not the end of Christ but the fulfillment of his mission.

29.synthesis: Otherwise, what do people mean by being baptized on behalf of the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why are people baptized on their behalf? Why are Paul and his companions in danger every hour? He dies every day. If he fought with beasts at Ephesus for purely human reasons, what does he gain? If the dead are not raised, 'Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die' (1 Corinthians 15:29–32). Do not be deceived: 'Bad company ruins good morals.' Wake up from your drunken stupor, as is right, and do not go on sinning. For some have no knowledge of God—he says this to their shame (1 Corinthians 15:33–34).
29.historical_context: 'Baptism on behalf of the dead' (v.29) is the most puzzling reference in Paul's letters—over forty interpretations have been proposed. The most natural reading is vicarious baptism practiced by some in Corinth (not endorsed by Paul) on behalf of those who died unbaptized. Paul's argument is ad hominem: whatever they mean by it, the practice assumes bodily resurrection, undermining their denial. His own sufferings (danger, dying daily, beasts at Ephesus) are the experiential argument: the apostolic life of risk and suffering makes no sense without resurrection. The 'Let us eat and drink' quotation (from Isaiah 22:13) is the logical consequence of resurrection-denial: if death ends everything, hedonism is the only rational response. The 'bad company ruins good morals' (Menander, Thais, v.33) is a common proverb applied to the Corinthians' contact with those who deny resurrection.
29.literary_structure: The passage makes three arguments for the resurrection from consequences of its denial: (1) the practice of baptism for the dead is inconsistent with denial (v.29); (2) apostolic suffering is irrational without resurrection (vv.30–32); (3) resurrection-denial leads to hedonism (v.32b). The wake-up call (vv.33–34) applies the practical conclusion: those in Corinth who are denying resurrection are being corrupted by bad company into sinful patterns consistent with their denial. Resurrection is not an abstract theological question but one that determines how one lives.
29.theological_application: Paul's argument from apostolic suffering is one of the most personally revealing in his letters. He suffers daily, faces death constantly, and would have no rational basis for doing so if death were the end. The cross-shaped life of the apostle is intelligible only within an eschatological framework where resurrection vindicates the pattern of dying for the sake of others. The church must understand that its own call to sacrificial service is similarly grounded in resurrection hope: without it, the 'eat and drink' philosophy is not merely understandable but arguably correct. The 'some have no knowledge of God' (v.34) is a sobering diagnostic: denial of resurrection is not merely an intellectual error but a symptom of insufficient knowledge of the God who raises the dead. Knowing God rightly means knowing him as the one who creates from nothing and gives life to the dead.
29.christology: 'I die every day' (v.31) is Paul's description of apostolic existence shaped by the cross: the one who follows the crucified Lord participates in the pattern of his dying as a daily reality. The 'beasts at Ephesus' (v.32, whether literal or metaphorical for the fierce opposition Paul faced) represent the concrete dangers that a resurrection-shaped life requires. The christological logic is the same as Romans 8:17: 'if we suffer with him, we will also be glorified with him.' The present danger is not evidence that resurrection is false but that it is genuinely necessary for the apostolic life to make sense. Christ's resurrection is the future fact that makes present dying endurable—not by making it comfortable but by revealing it as the seed that will bear resurrection fruit.

35.synthesis: But someone will ask: 'How are the dead raised? With what kind of body do they come?' Foolish one! What you sow does not come to life unless it dies; what you sow is not the body that will be but a bare kernel of wheat or some other grain; God gives it a body as he has chosen, and to each kind of seed its own body (1 Corinthians 15:35–38). Not all flesh is the same—there is human, animal, bird, and fish flesh; there are heavenly bodies and earthly bodies; the sun, moon, and stars each have different glory (1 Corinthians 15:39–41). So it is with the resurrection of the dead: what is sown is perishable; what is raised is imperishable. It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power. It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body (1 Corinthians 15:42–44). 'The first man Adam became a living being'; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit. But it is not the spiritual that is first but the natural, and then the spiritual. The first man was from the earth, a man of dust; the second man is from heaven. As was the man of dust, so also are those who are of the dust; as is the man of heaven, so also are those who are of heaven. Just as they have borne the image of the man of dust, they shall also bear the image of the man of heaven (1 Corinthians 15:45–49).
35.historical_context: The 'How?' and 'What kind of body?' questions (v.35) reflect Platonic skepticism about bodily resurrection: if the body is the soul's prison, why would anyone want a new one? Paul's seed analogy (vv.36–38) is not a biological claim but a philosophical one: continuous identity through discontinuous transformation is already known in nature. The graduated-glory description (sun/moon/stars, vv.40–41) illustrates that 'body' does not mean identical physicality—different kinds of bodies have different kinds of glory. The 'spiritual body' (soma pneumatikon, v.44) is not a non-material body but a body wholly animated and governed by the Spirit, in contrast to the 'natural body' (soma psychikon) animated by the soul in its present state. The last Adam/second man (v.45, 47) christology identifies Christ's resurrection body as the prototype of the transformed humanity that believers will become.
35.literary_structure: Paul answers the 'how' question with the seed analogy and the 'what kind' question with the graduated-glory illustrations. The four contrasts (perishable/imperishable; dishonor/glory; weakness/power; natural/spiritual, vv.42–44) describe the transformation without specifying its mechanism. The Adam-Christ typology (vv.45–49) provides the anthropological ground: the first Adam's image (dust, mortality, weakness) is what believers presently bear; the second Adam's image (glory, power, life) is what they will bear at the resurrection.
35.theological_application: The resurrection body theology has profound implications for Christian anthropology and ethics. The resurrection is not the escape from bodily existence but its transformation: what is sown in weakness and dishonor is raised in power and glory. This affirms the goodness of created embodied existence while acknowledging its current limitations and mortality. The church must hold this against both the dualism that denigrates the body (treating it as irrelevant to spiritual life) and the materialism that treats the present body as the final form (refusing to hope for transformation). The 'image of the man of heaven' (v.49) that believers will bear is the same image into which they are presently being transformed (cf. 2 Corinthians 3:18)—the resurrection is the completion of a transformation already begun in the Spirit's work of conforming believers to Christ's image.
35.christology: 'The last Adam became a life-giving spirit' (v.45) is one of the most compressed christological statements in Paul. Christ in his resurrection existence is now 'life-giving spirit'—not that he ceased to be bodily (the resurrection is bodily) but that in his risen state he is the source of the life-giving Spirit for those who belong to him. His resurrection body is the prototype: not resuscitation to the same mortal existence but transformation to the 'man of heaven' identity that defines the coming age. Believers who now bear the image of the man of dust (Adam) will at the resurrection bear the image of the man of heaven (Christ)—the same body transformed rather than discarded, now made fully alive to God, fully transparent to the Spirit, fully participant in the glory that Christ already possesses in his resurrection body.

50.synthesis: Flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does the perishable inherit the imperishable (1 Corinthians 15:50). Paul tells them a mystery: not all will sleep, but all will be changed—in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet, for the trumpet will sound and the dead will be raised imperishable, and they shall be changed (1 Corinthians 15:51–52). For this perishable body must put on the imperishable, and this mortal body must put on immortality. When the perishable puts on the imperishable and the mortal puts on immortality, then shall come to pass the saying: 'Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?' The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ (1 Corinthians 15:53–57). Therefore, beloved, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain (1 Corinthians 15:58).
50.historical_context: The 'flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom' (v.50) is not Platonic soul-escape but the statement that the present perishable body, in its mortality, is incapable of inhabiting the permanent eschatological order—not because bodies are bad but because mortality cannot enter immortality without transformation. The mystery (v.51) is the parousia scenario: some believers will be alive when Christ returns and will be transformed without dying. The Isaiah 25:8 ('death is swallowed up in victory') and Hosea 13:14 ('O death, where is your sting?') citations are applied to the resurrection as their ultimate fulfillment—the death that seemed victorious over Israel and humanity is finally and completely reversed. The 'sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law' (v.56) compresses Paul's Romans argument: sin uses the law to produce death; Christ's resurrection breaks that cycle at its root.
50.literary_structure: The passage moves from the logical necessity of transformation (v.50) to the mystery of how it happens (vv.51–53) to the scriptural celebration of the victory (vv.54–57) to the practical ethical conclusion (v.58). The climactic 'thanks be to God' (v.57) is the doxological response to the entire chapter's argument—the same God who raised Christ gives victory over the last enemy. The ethical conclusion (v.58) grounds the entire resurrection argument in present practice: resurrection hope does not produce passive waiting but steadfast, abounding work for the Lord, knowing it is not futile.
50.theological_application: 'Your labor is not in vain' (v.58) is the most directly ethical statement in the chapter and connects the resurrection theology to daily Christian living. The resurrection means that nothing done in the Lord—no act of love, no prayer, no word of witness, no suffering endured, no service rendered—is lost. The present creation is not permanent, but good work done in and for the Lord is preserved into the new creation. This is the eschatological ground for perseverance: not because the present arrangement is good enough to maintain forever but because the Lord who will raise the dead is the Lord who values every act done in his name. The church's labor—congregational ministry, compassionate service, faithful discipleship, missionary proclamation—is not futile even when results are not visible, because the one who gives the victory over death is the one for whom the labor is done.
50.christology: 'Thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ' (v.57)—the victory over sin, law, and death is given, not achieved. It is given through the Lord Jesus Christ whose resurrection is the victory itself. The death that was swallowed up is the death Christ himself died; the victory that swallowed it is the resurrection by which God vindicated the one who took the curse upon himself. The taunt of death ('where is your sting?') is addressed to the defeated enemy—not as bravado but as the announcement of a battle whose outcome is already determined by the resurrection. The church's present labor is done in the light of that already-accomplished victory: 'in the Lord your labor is not in vain' means in the risen Lord whose resurrection has changed everything, including the ultimate destiny of those who serve him and of the creation in which they serve.
